quiz_questions.question_text: What is Newton's First Law also called?

options.text: The Law of Gravity
The Law of Acceleration
The Law of Action-Reaction
The Law of Inertia

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force. The answer is 'The Law of Inertia'. The other options — 'The Law of Acceleration', 'The Law of Action-Reaction', and 'The Law of Gravity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is inertia?

options.text: The resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion
The weight of an object
The speed of an object
The force needed to move an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia — The resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion. Key Concepts — Newton's First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force.

quiz_questions.question_text: If no net force acts on a moving object, it will:

options.text: Slow down and stop
Continue at constant velocity
Change direction
Speed up

quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'Continue at constant velocity'. The other options — 'Slow down and stop', 'Speed up', and 'Change direction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is static equilibrium?

options.text: An object in free fall
An object at rest with ΣF = 0
An object accelerating
An object moving at constant speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium Static equilibrium — Object is at rest and ΣF = 0. Key Concepts — Newton's First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bus brakes suddenly. Why do passengers lurch forward?

options.text: The bus pushes them forward
Air resistance pushes them
Gravity pulls them forward
Their bodies' inertia keeps them moving forward

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest stays at rest, and a moving object keeps moving, unless acted on by a net force (Newton's First Law). The answer is 'Their bodies' inertia keeps them moving forward'. The other options — 'The bus pushes them forward', 'Gravity pulls them forward', and 'Air resistance pushes them' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines how much inertia an object has?

options.text: Its temperature
Its color
Its speed
Its mass

quiz_questions.explanation: More mass = more inertia. Key Concepts — Newton's First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force. The other options — 'Its speed', 'Its color', and 'Its temperature' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can an object be in equilibrium while moving?

options.text: Only at very low speeds
No, equilibrium requires rest
Only in space
Yes — this is called dynamic equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: An object is in mechanical equilibrium when the net force is zero (ΣF = 0). For static equilibrium, it's also at rest. For translational equilibrium, velocity is constant. The answer is 'Yes — this is called dynamic equilibrium'. The other options — 'No, equilibrium requires rest', 'Only in space', and 'Only at very low speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Newton's First Law, an object at rest will remain at rest unless:

options.text: Its temperature changes significantly
A net external force acts on it
Gravity is removed from the area
It is placed on a smooth surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's First Law states that an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity unless a net external force acts on it. This is the definition of inertia — objects resist changes to their state of motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which object has the greatest inertia?

options.text: A tennis ball with a mass of 0.06 kg
A baseball with a mass of 0.15 kg
A bowling ball with a mass of 6.0 kg
A basketball with a mass of 0.6 kg

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia depends on mass — the more massive an object is, the more it resists changes in motion. The bowling ball at 6.0 kg has far more mass than the other objects, so it has the greatest inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hockey puck slides across frictionless ice. What happens to its velocity?

options.text: It gradually slows down and stops
It stays exactly the same forever
It gradually speeds up over time
It changes direction on its own

quiz_questions.explanation: On frictionless ice, no net force acts on the puck. By Newton's First Law, the puck continues at the same speed and in the same direction indefinitely. Without a net force, there is no acceleration and no change in velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of Newton's First Law of Motion?

options.text: An object keeps its state of motion unless a net force acts on it
The acceleration of an object equals the net force divided by mass
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction force
Energy cannot be created or destroyed in a closed system

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's First Law states that an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force. The other options describe Newton's Second Law, Third Law, and conservation of energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'inertia' in your own words.

options.text: The tendency of an object to resist any change in its motion
The rate at which an object increases its speed over time
The amount of gravitational pull on an object near Earth
The force needed to move an object across a rough surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the natural tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest tends to stay at rest, and an object in motion tends to keep moving at the same speed and direction. Mass is the measure of inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'constant velocity' mean?

options.text: The object is not moving at all and is completely at rest
The object moves with unchanging speed in an unchanging direction
The object is speeding up at a steady rate each second
The object is slowing down but maintaining the same direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means both the speed and the direction of motion remain the same. If either speed or direction changes, the velocity is not constant. An object with constant velocity has zero acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'net external force'?

options.text: The overall combined force from all outside forces on an object
Any single force that is applied internally within an object
The force of gravity acting on an object near the surface
The maximum amount of friction between two surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: The net external force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object from outside sources. If external forces balance out, the net external force is zero and the object's motion does not change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dynamic equilibrium' mean?

options.text: An object is at rest with no forces acting on it at all
An object is accelerating due to an unbalanced net force
An object moves at constant velocity with zero net force
An object is vibrating rapidly while staying in one place

quiz_questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium describes a moving object with zero net force. The object moves at constant velocity — it does not speed up, slow down, or change direction. This contrasts with static equilibrium, where the object is at rest with zero net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'state of motion' refer to?

options.text: Whether an object is at rest or moving and at what velocity
The total number of forces currently acting on an object
The physical state of matter such as solid, liquid, or gas
The amount of energy an object has stored inside of it

quiz_questions.explanation: An object's state of motion describes whether it is at rest or in motion, and if moving, its speed and direction (velocity). Newton's First Law says an object's state of motion does not change unless a net force acts on it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by the phrase 'an object in motion stays in motion'?

options.text: A moving object will keep going at the same velocity without a net force
A moving object will always speed up unless something slows it down
Objects can never be brought to rest once they start moving
All moving objects eventually stop because of their own inertia

quiz_questions.explanation: This phrase from Newton's First Law means that a moving object continues at the same speed and in the same direction unless a net external force changes its motion. On Earth, forces like friction and air resistance usually slow objects down, but the law describes what happens in the absence of net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'static equilibrium'?

options.text: An object moving at constant velocity with balanced forces acting on it
An object at rest with zero net force so it remains stationary
An object falling freely under the influence of gravity alone
An object that is accelerating upward against the pull of gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: Static equilibrium means an object is at rest and the net force on it is zero (ΣF = 0). The object has no acceleration and no velocity. A book sitting on a table is a common example of static equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resistance to change' mean in the context of inertia?

options.text: An object opposes any attempt to alter its current state of motion
An object can never be moved once it is placed at rest anywhere
An object always moves in the opposite direction of applied force
An object naturally speeds up when it encounters any kind of friction

quiz_questions.explanation: In the context of inertia, resistance to change means an object tends to keep doing what it is already doing. A stationary object resists being set in motion, and a moving object resists being stopped or redirected. Greater mass means greater resistance to change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'net force equals zero' tell you about an object?

options.text: The object must be accelerating in some direction right now
There are absolutely no forces acting on the object at all
The object has zero acceleration so its velocity is not changing
The object is losing energy and will soon come to a full stop

quiz_questions.explanation: When the net force is zero, the object has zero acceleration. This means its velocity is not changing — it is either at rest or moving at constant velocity. Multiple forces can still act on the object; they simply balance each other out.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is standing on a bus that suddenly starts moving forward. The student stumbles backward. Which concept best explains why?

options.text: Gravity increased when the bus started accelerating forward
The student's inertia kept the body at rest while the bus moved
The bus applied an extra backward force on the student's body
Air resistance from outside the bus pushed the student backward

quiz_questions.explanation: The student's body was at rest when the bus started. Due to inertia, the body resisted the change in motion and tended to stay in place while the bus accelerated forward underneath, making the student appear to stumble backward relative to the bus.

quiz_questions.question_text: A magician pulls a tablecloth quickly from under a set of dishes. The dishes barely move. Which principle explains this?

options.text: Newton's Second Law: the dishes have a large net force on them
Newton's First Law: the dishes' inertia keeps them in place
Newton's Third Law: the cloth pushes the dishes in reverse direction
Conservation of energy: no energy transfers to the dishes at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The dishes are at rest and have inertia. When the cloth is pulled quickly, friction acts on the dishes for only a very short time, so the force is not enough to overcome their inertia. The dishes tend to stay at rest, demonstrating Newton's First Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a coin on top of a card over a glass. She flicks the card away quickly, and the coin drops into the glass. Why does the coin fall straight down?

options.text: The card pushes the coin downward as it is removed quickly
The coin's inertia kept it in place while the card was removed
Air rushing in below the card creates suction that pulls the coin
The glass creates a magnetic field that attracts the metal coin

quiz_questions.explanation: The coin was at rest on the card. When the card was flicked away quickly, friction between card and coin acted for too short a time to move the coin sideways. The coin's inertia kept it in place, and gravity then pulled it straight down into the glass.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a car crash test, a dummy flies forward when the car stops suddenly. A student asks why this happens. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The engine pushes the dummy forward when it shuts off abruptly
The dashboard creates a pulling force on the dummy's upper body
The dummy's inertia keeps it moving forward even as the car stops
Gravity suddenly increases during the impact and pulls the dummy ahead

quiz_questions.explanation: The dummy was moving forward along with the car. When the car stopped suddenly, the dummy's inertia caused it to continue moving forward. This is exactly why seatbelts are important — they apply a force to stop the passenger along with the car.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student rides a skateboard at constant speed on a flat, smooth road. If no additional forces act on the student, what will happen?

options.text: The student will gradually speed up due to the slope of the road
The student will keep moving at the same speed in the same direction
The student will slow down because all objects naturally stop moving
The student will change direction because of Earth's magnetic field

quiz_questions.explanation: With no additional forces acting, Newton's First Law tells us the student will continue at the same speed and direction. Objects do not naturally slow down on their own — on Earth, friction and air resistance are the forces that slow things down.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shakes a bottle of ketchup upside down and then suddenly stops. The ketchup keeps moving out of the bottle. Why?

options.text: Air pressure inside the bottle pushes the ketchup out quickly
The ketchup's inertia keeps it moving after the bottle stops
Gravity pulls harder on ketchup than it does on the glass bottle
Chemical reactions inside the bottle create a force that ejects it

quiz_questions.explanation: When the student shakes the bottle downward and then stops, the ketchup inside was also moving downward. Due to inertia, the ketchup continues to move even after the bottle is brought to a stop — it resists the change in motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student places a heavy block and a light block on identical frictionless surfaces and pushes each with the same force for the same time. Which block is harder to get moving?

options.text: The light block because it has less surface area on the table
Both blocks are equally difficult to set in motion on the surface
The heavy block because it has more mass and therefore more inertia
Neither block is hard to move since the surfaces are frictionless

quiz_questions.explanation: The heavy block has more mass, which means more inertia and more resistance to changes in motion. Even on a frictionless surface, a greater force or longer time is needed to achieve the same change in velocity for a heavier object.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is ice skating in a straight line at constant speed. She stops pushing with her legs. Assuming the ice is nearly frictionless, what happens next?

options.text: She immediately stops because there is no force pushing her forward
She speeds up because ice has a special accelerating property
She continues gliding at nearly the same speed and direction
She starts moving in a curved path instead of a straight line

quiz_questions.explanation: With negligible friction, no net force acts on the skater after she stops pushing. Newton's First Law tells us she will continue moving at approximately the same speed and in the same direction — her inertia keeps her gliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spacecraft in deep space, far from any planet or star, fires its engines briefly and then shuts them off. What does the spacecraft do after the engines turn off?

options.text: It gradually slows down and eventually drifts to a complete stop
It continues at the same velocity because there is no net force
It speeds up because the leftover fuel keeps pushing it forward
It spins in circles because there is nothing to hold it straight

quiz_questions.explanation: In deep space there is virtually no friction, air resistance, or significant gravitational pull. Once the engines shut off, there is no net force on the spacecraft, so by Newton's First Law it keeps moving at constant velocity indefinitely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student stacks books on a desk. Each book remains at rest on the stack. Why don't the books accelerate?

options.text: The books have no forces acting on them while they sit still
Gravity pushes the books upward and the desk pushes them down
The forces on each book are balanced so the net force is zero
The books are too heavy for any force to make them accelerate

quiz_questions.explanation: Each book has gravity pulling it down and a normal force from the book below (or the desk) pushing it up. These forces are equal and opposite, so the net force on each book is zero. By Newton's First Law, the books remain at rest — they are in static equilibrium.
